key_requirements: os.makedirs spec says 'must not error if directory already exists (equivalent to exist_ok=True behavior)' — CPython 3.12 signature is makedirs(name, mode=0o777, exist_ok=False); the spec bakes in exist_ok=True but must expose it as an optional parameter defaulting to False
os.listdir spec: 'excludes . and ..' — CPython 3.12 signature is listdir(path='.') where path can be str, bytes, or path-like object; current spec only shows str path

os.stat return type: spec says 'return file metadata (size, mtime)' — CPython returns os.stat_result with st_mode, st_ino, st_dev, st_nlink, st_uid, st_gid, st_size, st_atime, st_mtime, st_ctime; current spec only mentions size+mtime

os.environ: spec mentions __getitem__, __setitem__, get, keys — CPython Mapping also has __contains__, __delitem__, items, values, pop, setdefault, update; missing setenv/delenv via environ assignment
Missing CPython 3.12 os functions: os.getpid(), os.getuid(), os.access(), os.chmod(), os.symlink(), os.readlink(), os.scandir() — these are part of the standard API surface
os.path.realpath() is missing from spec (present in CPython as alias for abspath with symlink resolution); also missing os.path.relpath(), os.path.expanduser(), os.path.expandvars()
Error types: spec specifies FileNotFoundError for os.remove/os.rename on nonexistent — CPython 3.12 also raises PermissionError for permission denied, IsADirectoryError for directories

sys.exit() return type: spec correctly says 'raises SystemExit' — but CPython 3.12 also documents that if code is a non-integer, non-string type (e.g., a list), SystemExit.code is set to that object; current spec only handles int and str cases

Missing sys attributes: sys.byteorder ('little'/'big'), sys.builtin_module_names (tuple of built-in module names), sys.executable (path to interpreter), sys.flags (namedtuple), sys.float_info, sys.int_info, sys.version_info (namedtuple with major/minor/micro/releaselevel/serial)
Missing sys functions: sys.getrecursionlimit(), sys.setrecursionlimit(n), sys.getrefcount(obj), sys.getsizeof(obj), sys.intern(string) — all standard in CPython 3.12
sys.modules is mentioned in spec as 'a dict mapping module names to module objects' — CPython 3.12 modules can be deleted from sys.modules to force reimport; this side-effect must be supported
sys.stdin/stdout/stderr: spec says 'object' type — CPython 3.12 these are TextIOWrapper with write(), readline(), flush(), fileno() methods; current spec does not verify their interface
sys.version: spec says 'Mamba version string' but should report 3.12-compatible version string for conformance tools that parse sys.version

json.dumps signature gap: spec shows json.dumps(obj, indent=None) but CPython 3.12 signature is dumps(obj, *, skipkeys=False, ensure_ascii=True, check_circular=True, allow_nan=True, cls=None, indent=None, separators=None, default=None, sort_keys=False, **kw); at minimum sort_keys and ensure_ascii are commonly used

json.loads signature gap: spec shows json.loads(s) but CPython 3.12 is loads(s, *, cls=None, object_hook=None, parse_float=None, parse_int=None, parse_constant=None, object_pairs_hook=None, **kw); parse_float and parse_int are used in many applications

json.JSONDecodeError: spec says 'raise json.JSONDecodeError (or ValueError)' — CPython 3.12 json.JSONDecodeError is a subclass of ValueError with attributes .msg, .doc, .pos, .lineno, .colno; the error type must be json.JSONDecodeError specifically, not generic ValueError
json.load() and json.dump() (file-based) are not in the spec but are standard CPython 3.12 API; commonly used with open() files

Float serialization: spec does not address NaN and Infinity — CPython 3.12 with allow_nan=True (default) serializes float('nan') as NaN, float('inf') as Infinity (non-standard JSON but CPython compatible); with allow_nan=False these raise ValueError

Dict key coercion: spec says 'Dict keys must be strings; non-string keys raise TypeError' — but CPython 3.12 actually coerces int/float/bool/None keys to their string representations by default unless skipkeys=False raises TypeError

All module-level functions missing flags parameter: CPython 3.12 signatures include flags=0 for search/match/fullmatch/findall/finditer/sub/subn/split/compile; without flags, re.IGNORECASE / re.MULTILINE / re.DOTALL cannot be used

re.sub count parameter: spec mentions 'count parameter limits the number of replacements' but actual CPython 3.12 signature is sub(pattern, repl, string, count=0, flags=0); count=0 means replace all (not 0 replacements); must verify Mamba implements count=0 as 'replace all'
re.split maxsplit: spec mentions maxsplit but CPython 3.12 signature is split(pattern, string, maxsplit=0, flags=0) where maxsplit=0 means no limit; must verify maxsplit=0 behavior

re.compile return type: compiled pattern object must support .pattern attribute (the original pattern string), .flags attribute (int), .groups (number of groups), .groupindex (dict) — these are part of the CPython 3.12 compiled pattern API

Match object missing attributes: spec shows m.group/groups/start/end/span but CPython 3.12 Match also has .string (input string), .re (compiled pattern), .pos/.endpos (search boundaries), .lastindex, .lastgroup

re.escape return type: CPython 3.12 re.escape(pattern) — in Python 3.7+ only special regex chars are escaped (not all non-alphanumerics); must verify Mamba matches this behavior, not old behavior that escaped more characters
re.IGNORECASE, re.MULTILINE, re.DOTALL, re.VERBOSE constants must be exported from the re module as integer constants

Counter missing operations: CPython 3.12 Counter has subtract(iterable_or_mapping), total() (3.10+), __or__ / __and__ / __pos__ / __neg__ / __iadd__ / __isub__; spec only shows most_common, elements, update, +/-

namedtuple is a core collections type completely absent from the spec: CPython 3.12 collections.namedtuple(typename, field_names) returns a new class; _make, _asdict, _replace, _fields are standard; this is heavily used in stdlib
deque.extend/extendleft/clear/copy/count/index/remove are missing from spec: CPython 3.12 deque has all list-like methods; spec only shows append/appendleft/pop/popleft/rotate
Counter.most_common(n) with n=None: spec says 'Top n elements as (elem, count)' but when n is None CPython returns all elements sorted by count descending; must verify None case works
OrderedDict CPython 3.12 API gap: spec says 'alias' but OrderedDict has move_to_end(key, last=True), popitem(last=True) which differ from plain dict; required for code that uses these methods
collections.abc and ChainMap/UserDict/UserList/UserString are part of collections module namespace in CPython 3.12; absence of these means imports like 'from collections import ChainMap' fail
itertools.pairwise(iterable) added in Python 3.10 is not in spec: returns sliding pairs (a,b),(b,c),(c,d)...; included in CPython 3.12 conformance scope
itertools.batched(iterable, n) added in Python 3.12 is not in spec: yields non-overlapping batches of up to n items; this is a 3.12-specific addition that must be included for full 3.12 conformance

islice behavior gap: spec shows islice(iterable, stop) and islice(iterable, start, stop, step) but doesn't address CPython behavior when stop=None (means iterate to end); must verify islice(iter, 2, None) works

accumulate initial parameter: spec shows accumulate(iterable, func=add) but CPython 3.12 signature is accumulate(iterable, func=operator.add, *, initial=None); when initial is provided, it's prepended to the accumulation; missing from spec

groupby key=None behavior: spec says 'Group consecutive equal elements' but with key=None CPython groups by identity (each element is its own key); key function behavior must be verified for common cases
tee(iterable, n=2): spec describes this but doesn't address thread safety (CPython 3.12 tee is NOT thread-safe) or memory implications when iterators are consumed at different rates
filterfalse with pred=None: CPython 3.12 allows pred=None which means filter out falsy values (like built-in filter(None, ...)); must verify this case
lru_cache decorator usage: spec shows @lru_cache(maxsize=128) but Python 3.8+ allows @lru_cache without parentheses (maxsize defaults to 128); must verify bare @lru_cache works in Mamba

partial return type: spec says 'returned object has func, args, keywords attributes' — CPython 3.12 partial object also has __call__, __repr__, and __eq__ defined; must verify keywords attribute returns {} not None for no-kwargs case

wraps missing __wrapped__: spec says wraps copies __name__, __doc__, __qualname__, __dict__, __wrapped__ but __wrapped__ is set to the original function — must verify __wrapped__ chain works for decorator introspection

total_ordering missing __ne__: spec says 'class must define __eq__ and at least one ordering method' but doesn't mention that __ne__ is NOT automatically derived by total_ordering (CPython relies on object's default __ne__ = not __eq__)
cached_property thread safety: CPython 3.12 cached_property is NOT thread-safe for concurrent first access; spec doesn't document this; must not falsely imply thread safety

reduce with initial=None: spec says 'When initial is provided' but doesn't address that initial=None is a valid explicit initial value distinct from no initial argument — the sentinel pattern in CPython must be preserved
functools.singledispatch decorator is not in spec but is a standard CPython 3.12 functools export; commonly used in codebases
math.comb(n, k) and math.perm(n, k=None) added in Python 3.8 are not in spec but are standard in CPython 3.12; math.comb for combinatorics is commonly used
math.prod(iterable, *, start=1) added in Python 3.8 is not in spec: returns product of all elements; standard CPython 3.12 function
math.isclose(a, b, *, rel_tol=1e-09, abs_tol=0.0) not in spec: essential for float comparison with tolerance; commonly used in testing
math.log base argument: spec shows math.log(x) for natural log only, but CPython 3.12 signature is log(x, base=math.e) allowing log(x, 10) as alternative to log10(x); two-arg form must work
math.floor/ceil return type: CPython 3.12 math.floor/ceil return int (not float); spec says 'largest integer <= x' which is correct semantically but must verify return type is int not float

math.factorial argument: CPython 3.12 accepts any non-negative integer but raises ValueError for floats that are not integral (e.g., math.factorial(5.0) raises ValueError in 3.12); spec should note this change from earlier Python behavior
math.tau constant (= 2*pi) not in spec; math.tau added in Python 3.6, standard in 3.12; also math.inf and math.nan are in spec but math.tau is missing

random.seed() signature gap: spec shows random.seed(n) for integer n only; CPython 3.12 signature is seed(a=None, version=2) where a can be int, float, str, bytes, bytearray, or None (uses os.urandom); version parameter affects seeding for str/bytes
random.choices() not in spec: CPython 3.12 random.choices(population, weights=None, *, cum_weights=None, k=1) returns k elements with replacement; weighted sampling is commonly used
random.sample() with counts parameter: CPython 3.12 signature is sample(population, k, *, counts=None); counts allows sampling from a population with repetition counts; missing from spec

random.shuffle() return type: spec says 'shuffles the list in place, returns None' — CPython 3.12 random.shuffle(x) takes just x (population parameter was removed in 3.11); signature change must be verified
random.Random class: CPython 3.12 exposes random.Random as a class for creating independent random instances; only module-level functions in spec

PRNG algorithm: spec says 'xorshift64' but CPython 3.12 uses Mersenne Twister (MT19937); output values will differ from CPython for same seeds; for tests that rely on specific PRNG output this matters
random.randbytes(n) added in Python 3.9: returns n random bytes; standard CPython 3.12 function not in spec

datetime.fromisoformat() not in spec: CPython 3.12 datetime.fromisoformat(date_string) parses ISO 8601 format; in 3.11+ it handles full ISO 8601 including timezone offsets (extended beyond the pre-3.11 limited behavior)
datetime.fromtimestamp() not in spec: CPython 3.12 datetime.fromtimestamp(timestamp, tz=None) is the primary way to create datetime from Unix timestamp; spec only shows dt.timestamp() (the reverse)
date.fromisoformat() not in spec: CPython 3.12 date.fromisoformat(date_string) parses 'YYYY-MM-DD'; commonly used
datetime.replace() not in spec: CPython 3.12 dt.replace(year, month, day, hour, minute, second, microsecond, tzinfo) returns new datetime with replaced fields; heavily used
timedelta arithmetic: spec shows arithmetic operations correctly but doesn't mention that timedelta supports floor division (//) and modulo (%) with other timedeltas (added Python 3.x)

timezone handling: spec has no mention of timezone-aware datetimes (tzinfo, datetime.timezone.utc); CPython 3.12 datetime.now(tz=None) with tz parameter is important; datetime.utcnow() was deprecated in 3.12

datetime.__format__() / format(dt, fmt): spec shows strftime() but CPython 3.12 also supports format(dt, '%Y-%m-%d') and f'{dt:%Y-%m-%d}' via __format__; same strftime codes must work in format context
Path.open() not in spec: CPython 3.12 p.open(mode='r', buffering=-1, encoding=None, errors=None, newline=None) is the pathlib equivalent of open(); very commonly used
Path.touch(mode=0o666, exist_ok=True) not in spec: creates file if not exists, updates mtime if exists
Path.symlink_to(target, target_is_directory=False) and Path.is_symlink() not in spec: important for filesystem operations
Path.relative_to(other) not in spec: CPython 3.12 p.relative_to('/usr') returns Path('bin/python') for p=Path('/usr/bin/python')
Path comparison operators: CPython 3.12 Path objects support ==, < (lexicographic) for comparison; spec doesn't mention this
Path.read_bytes() not in spec: complementary to read_text() for binary file reading; returns bytes object
Path.write_bytes(data) not in spec: complementary to write_text() for binary writing
Path.home() class method not in spec: CPython 3.12 Path.home() returns Path to current user's home directory; commonly used
Path.cwd() class method not in spec: returns Path of current working directory (Path-based equivalent of os.getcwd())
StringIO.readline() and StringIO.readlines() not in spec: CPython 3.12 IOBase protocol requires readline(size=-1) and readlines(hint=-1); needed for line-by-line reading patterns
StringIO.truncate(size=None) not in spec: CPython 3.12 truncates stream at current position or given size
io.open() function not in spec: CPython 3.12 io.open() is the underlying implementation of built-in open(); same signature; io module re-exports it
io.DEFAULT_BUFFER_SIZE constant not in spec: CPython 3.12 exposes this as 8192

pprint.pformat depth parameter: spec shows pformat(obj, indent=1, width=80) but CPython 3.12 signature is pformat(object, indent=1, width=80, depth=None, *, compact=False, sort_dicts=True, underscore_numbers=False); depth limits nesting levels printed
csv.DictReader and csv.DictWriter not in spec: CPython 3.12 csv module's DictReader/DictWriter map rows to/from dicts; heavily used in real code
csv.QUOTE_ALL, csv.QUOTE_MINIMAL, csv.QUOTE_NONNUMERIC, csv.QUOTE_NONE constants not in spec: these control quoting behavior in csv.writer
BytesIO.read1() not in spec: CPython 3.12 BytesIO has read1() method inherited from BufferedIOBase

usedforsecurity parameter: CPython 3.12 hashlib constructors have usedforsecurity=True parameter (added in 3.9) to indicate whether the hash is used for security purposes; affects FIPS compliance; current spec doesn't mention this
hashlib.sha3_256, hashlib.sha3_512, hashlib.blake2b, hashlib.blake2s not in spec: CPython 3.12 includes SHA-3 family and BLAKE2; sha3 is the recommended algorithm for new code
h.block_size attribute not in spec: CPython 3.12 hash objects expose block_size (block size in bytes) alongside digest_size; e.g., SHA-256 block_size is 64
hashlib.file_digest(fileobj, digest) added in Python 3.11 not in spec: efficiently hashes file content; part of CPython 3.12 API
hashlib.pbkdf2_hmac(hash_name, password, salt, iterations, dklen=None) not in spec: standard CPython 3.12 PBKDF2 key derivation function; used in password hashing
hashlib.scrypt(password, salt, n, r, p, maxmem=0, dklen=64) not in spec: added in CPython 3.6, standard in 3.12
h.copy() method not in spec: CPython 3.12 hash objects support copy() to checkpoint hash state; important for incremental hashing patterns

Current native backends: serde_json (json), regex crate (re), chrono (datetime/time), rusqlite (sqlite3), sha2/md-5/rand — these provide the Rust-level implementations that the stdlib audit must verify against CPython 3.12

Error mapping: spec defines a unified error mapping layer from Rust crate errors to Mamba exceptions; this is the mechanism by which json.JSONDecodeError, re.error, ValueError, etc. are raised — must verify error types match CPython 3.12 exactly

Registration flow: stdlib modules are registered via register_stdlib() → each module's register() → module_registry.register_module() → symbols.rs define_function(); changes to function signatures require updating the registered function pointer AND the module attribute

Stubs vs implementations: the spec acknowledges that many modules were 'stubs or basic manual implementations'; the api conformance work must identify which functions are still stubs vs. properly implemented and prioritize fixing implemented ones first

serde_json for json: CPython json.loads/dumps behavior for NaN/Infinity, integer key coercion, and non-serializable type errors must be verified against serde_json's behavior (serde_json is stricter by default)
regex crate for re: the regex crate does NOT support lookahead/lookbehind assertions or backreferences; this is a known API gap vs CPython's re module which uses PCRE-compatible syntax

R11 in conformance spec: 18 stdlib xfail fixture files across 12 modules (collections, csv, datetime, functools, hashlib, io, itertools, json, math, random, re, struct) — these fixtures need to be updated to test the actual implemented API, matching CPython 3.12 golden output

Stdlib fixture locations: flat fixtures at conformance/stdlib/{module}_conformance.py AND nested at conformance/stdlib/{module}/{module}_conformance.py — both must be updated; flat paths currently contain minimal smoke tests (just 'import X; print(isinstance(X, object))')

Golden file regeneration: after fixing API signatures, regen_golden.py must be run with CPython 3.12 to regenerate .expected files; all stdlib tests must produce output that CPython 3.12 would produce

Test strategy from conformance spec R11: 'Simplify to test only implemented features' vs issue #760 which wants 'audit and fix mismatches' — the two specs complement each other: fix the implementations, then test them (not simplify to avoid testing them)

xfail directive: conformance test files with '# mamba-xfail' are expected to fail; for stdlib conformance, the goal is to remove these markers by fixing the implementations, not just by removing the tests

Current xfail stdlib modules from test matrix: math_basic.py (simplify), json_conformance.py (simplify), re_conformance.py (simplify), itertools_conformance.py (simplify), collections_conformance.py (simplify), etc. — these are currently failing because the API implementations don't match CPython 3.12

Complementary P2 conformance work covers nested f-strings (P2-R1), metaclass= forwarding (P2-R2), descriptor protocol (P2-R3) — these are language-level features distinct from stdlib API conformance (#760)

Same test infrastructure: conformance_tests.rs harness used for P2 fixtures is the same harness that stdlib conformance tests run through — same fixture format (.py + .expected golden file), same verification commands
Regression constraint: 'All existing passing conformance tests must continue to pass' — stdlib API fixes in #760 must not break passing language tests from P2 work or other conformance tests

Harness dispatch: fixture .py files with '# RUN: jit' directive are JIT-compiled and executed; stdout is compared against .expected golden file via byte-for-byte comparison; stdlib conformance tests use jit mode

Golden file format: .expected files contain exact stdout output with newlines; any difference in float formatting, exception message wording, or whitespace causes failure — CPython 3.12 must be used to regenerate
Fixture discovery: recursive .py file discovery under tests/fixtures/; new stdlib API test files can be added to tests/fixtures/conformance/stdlib/ subdirectories and will be auto-discovered
The P1 lang features spec covers #847/#845/#846/#835/#848 (decorators, star expressions, global/nonlocal, slice step, string escapes) — these are distinct from stdlib conformance #760

bytes type from #848 is relevant: the bytes type (b'...') affects how stdlib modules like hashlib (which takes bytes), re (bytes patterns), and io (BytesIO) interoperate with the runtime; bytes implementation status from #848 affects stdlib conformance
